html:
Q 33:36 — "no choice" once Allah and His Messenger have decided Governance Moral Problems Women Logical Inconsistency Strong Quran 33:36
"It is not for a believing man or a believing woman, when Allah and His Messenger have decided a matter, that they should [thereafter] have any choice about their affair. And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger has certainly strayed into clear error."

What the verse says

Once Allah and His Messenger have decided any matter, the believer has no choice in their own affairs regarding that matter. Disobedience is "clear error." The verse appears immediately before the Zaynab marriage episode (33:37) and classical tafsir reads it as requiring Zaynab's compliance.

Why this is a problem

  1. Moral autonomy is foreclosed by definition — the believer's "obedience" is no longer a moral act, it is the absence of an option to choose otherwise. The scope is unlimited: "any choice about their affair."
  2. Modern Salafi and Islamist movements cite Q 33:36 explicitly as proof that constitutional democracy — reserving a legislative sphere for human discretion — is theologically illegitimate.

Why it fails

The verse contains no qualifier — its grammar is universal: any matter Allah and His Messenger have decided. Once a domain is ruled on, the no-choice clause activates, licensing unlimited expansion of religious authority into personal life. Modern Islamist movements have used precisely this expansion logic, reading the verse on its plain terms.

Q 47:35 — "do not call for peace while you are superior" Warfare & Jihad Prophetic Character Moral Problems Governance Strong Quran 47:35
"So do not weaken and call for peace while you are superior (al-a'lawn); and Allah is with you and will never deprive you of [the reward of] your deeds."

What the verse says

Muslims in a position of military superiority must not initiate peace overtures. Peace from strength is framed as weakness. Allah promises continued reward for not calling for peace.

Why this is a problem

  1. Modern conflict ethics converge: the strong have a special duty to seek peace. Q 47:35 commands the opposite — and produced the classical doctrine that Muslim states may conclude only temporary truces (hudna) when weak.
  2. Sayyid Qutb's commentary and al-Qaeda/ISIS literature cite Q 47:35 as the canonical refutation of Muslim-government peace processes with non-Muslim states.

Why it fails

The hudna-only-when-weak doctrine is built directly on Q 47:35 and operated as Sharia governance for over a millennium. Egypt-Israel and Jordan-Israel peace treaties were denounced by classical-trained scholars citing this verse. Allah's explicit promise not to deprive believers of their deeds if they do not call for peace makes the prohibition theologically reinforced, not mere tactical advice.

Q 12:40 — "legislation is for Allah alone" is the canonical theocracy proof-text Governance Moral Problems Logical Inconsistency Pre-Islamic Borrowings Strong Quran 12:40
"Legislation is not but for Allah (in al-hukmu illa lillah). He has commanded that you worship not except Him. That is the correct religion, but most of the people do not know."

What the verse says

Joseph's prison sermon includes the clause "all legislative authority belongs to Allah alone." The phrase appears three times in the Quran (6:57, 12:40, 12:67) and became the foundational proof-text for Islamic governance theory across classical and modern periods.

Why this is a problem

  1. Read literally, no human legislature has standing to enact laws not derived from divine revelation — the position the Muslim Brotherhood, Saudi establishment, Iranian theocracy, and Salafi-Jihadist movements all hold.
  2. The Khawarij coined "la hukma illa lillah" in 657 CE to denounce Ali's acceptance of human arbitration — the verse has been a rebellion-warrant from Islam's first decade, through to Sayyid Qutb's Milestones (1964).

Why it fails

1,400 years of Muslim political movements have taken the abstracted reading as canonical — the Khawarij, Wahhabis, Brotherhood, ISIS. Saying they "misread" concedes that the canonical hermeneutic is largely on the literalist side. The siyasa shar'iyyah framework cannot accommodate the pluralistic legislative latitude modern democracies require.

Q 9:14–15 — Allah punishes unbelievers "by your hands" and killing satisfies believers' hearts Warfare & Jihad Prophetic Character Allah's Character Moral Problems Strong Quran 9:14–15
"Fight them; Allah will punish them by your hands and will disgrace them and give you victory over them and satisfy the breasts of a believing people. And remove the fury in the believers' hearts."

What the verse says

Muslim military violence is Allah's chosen instrument for punishing unbelievers. The verse identifies three benefits of the killing: victory, satisfaction of believers' emotional state (sudur), and removal of believers' fury (ghayz).

Why this is a problem

  1. Killing is framed as emotional therapy — the verse explicitly describes combat as catharsis for the believer performing it.
  2. "By your hands" removes the restraint a separate divine punishment would imply — the believer who hesitates is failing cosmic instrumentality. Salafi-Jihadist literature cites Q 9:14 as the canonical instrumental-violence verse, which is a plain reading, not a distortion.

Why it fails

Classical tafsir (Tabari, Ibn Kathir, Qurtubi) treated Q 9:14 as generalisable doctrine. The "channels anger" gloss does not change the verse's content: anger-relief through killing is a divinely-promised consequence of obedience. Modern reformist disavowal is welcome moral progress; it is not the canonical reading of the text.

Q 4:59 + 4:65 — obey Allah, Messenger, and rulers; find no discomfort from the Messenger's judgment Governance Moral Problems Logical Inconsistency Prophetic Character Strong Quran 4:59–65
"O you who have believed, obey Allah and obey the Messenger and those in authority (uli al-amr) among you... they will not [truly] believe until they make you, [O Muhammad], judge... and then find within themselves no discomfort from what you have judged and submit in [full, willing] submission."

What the verse says

Q 4:59 places political rulers inside the divine-prophetic obedience chain. Q 4:65 requires not just external compliance but internal acceptance — no discomfort from the Prophet's rulings. Q 4:60 denounces those who refer disputes to taghut (non-Islamic authorities).

Why this is a problem

  1. "Find no discomfort" criminalises inner dissent — the modern distinction between outward behaviour and inner thought is explicitly collapsed.
  2. The taghut frame has been the canonical proof-text for declaring secular Muslim governments apostate — Qutb, Mawdudi, and Hizb ut-Tahrir all cite Q 4:60 directly.

Why it fails

Q 4:65's "no discomfort" demand cannot be defused without abandoning the verse's plain wording, which collapses the inner-outer distinction modern liberal religion requires. The taghut-narrowing is contradicted by salafi-jihadist scholarship that reads any non-Sharia government as taghut — a reading the verse's grammar supports.

Q 69:32 — seventy-cubit chain and wound-pus food, for not believing and not feeding the poor Hell Gross / Vile Allah's Character Moral Problems Strong Quran 69:30–37
"[Allah will say,] 'Seize him and shackle him. Then into Hellfire drive him. Then into a chain whose length is seventy cubits insert him.'... there is not for him here any food except from ghislin [the discharge of wounds]."

What the verse says

The condemned is shackled, driven to Hellfire, and threaded through a 70-cubit chain (~35 metres). His only food is ghislin — glossed by classical commentators as the pus-and-blood discharge from other Hellfire inhabitants. The triggering crimes: not believing in Allah and not encouraging the feeding of the poor.

Why this is a problem

  1. A cubit is a specific physical measurement (~45 cm); seventy cubits is dimensional reportage, not metaphor. Classical tafsir preserved the literal reading.
  2. Failure to advocate for charitable feeding is equated with disbelief as a cause of eternal torture — fusing economic conduct with creedal conformity at the threshold of infinite punishment.

Why it fails

"Concrete imagery for the audience" concedes that divine communication is calibrated to specific historical-cultural taste — which makes the timeless-revelation claim harder to defend. The disproportion problem stands: infinite torture for a finite failure to advocate for feeding the poor cannot be resolved by noting that both creed and charity were violated. The specific chain-length and food-type track 7th-century body-horror vocabulary, not eternal divine precision.

Allah seals disbelievers' hearts, then punishes them for disbelief Logical Inconsistency Strong Quran 2:6–7
"Indeed, those who disbelieve — it is all the same for them whether you warn them or do not warn them — they will not believe. Allah has set a seal upon their hearts and upon their hearing, and over their vision is a veil. And for them is a great punishment."

What the verse says

Allah Himself seals the hearts and ears of disbelievers and veils their sight. Because of this seal, they cannot believe. Then Allah punishes them for not believing.

Why this is a problem

The causal chain runs: Allah → seal → disbelief → punishment. Every link is caused by Allah, yet only the human is blamed. If Allah disables your ability to choose X, He cannot justly punish you for failing to choose X. This is not simple predestination — it is active divine sabotage followed by eternal torture for the resulting behaviour.

Why it fails

The verse gives no such sequence. It states the outcome ("they will not believe") and then gives the reason ("Allah has set a seal"). If the seal came after rejection, the verse would say so. A being who knows the future cannot be reacting rather than causing — and the plain grammar assigns the sealing as active divine action, not passive confirmation.

Salvation for Jews, Christians, Sabeans — then cancelled by Q 3:85 Contradiction Abrogation Strong Quran 2:62 vs 3:85
"Indeed, those who believed and those who were Jews or Christians or Sabeans — those [among them] who believed in Allah and the Last Day and did righteousness — will have their reward with their Lord..." (2:62)
"And whoever desires other than Islam as religion — never will it be accepted from him, and he, in the Hereafter, will be among the losers." (3:85)

What the verses say

Q 2:62 says righteous Jews, Christians, and Sabeans who believe in God and the Last Day will be saved. Q 3:85 says no religion other than Islam is ever accepted. The Saheeh International footnote on 2:62 explicitly resolves the contradiction by invoking abrogation: "After the coming of Prophet Muhammad no religion other than Islam is acceptable to Allah, as stated in 3:85."

Why this is a problem

An all-knowing, eternal God revealing universally true statements does not need to cancel earlier revelations. If 2:62 was true when Allah said it, it should still be true. If it was never true, Allah stated a falsehood. The translators' own footnote concedes the contradiction and reaches for abrogation — which is the admission, not the solution.

Why it fails

The verse makes no temporal qualification. The same Allah who specifies conditions throughout the Quran did not do so here. Adding a temporal condition post-hoc to preserve coherence is special pleading — inserting a qualification the verse does not contain, precisely because without it the contradiction is undeniable.

Angels Harut and Marut sent to Babylon to teach marriage-destroying magic Strange / Obscure Logical Inconsistency Strong Quran 2:102
"...that which was revealed to the two angels at Babylon, Harut and Marut. But they do not teach anyone unless they say, 'We are a trial, so do not disbelieve [by practicing magic].' And [yet] they learn from them that by which they cause separation between a man and his wife."

What the verse says

Two angels named Harut and Marut were sent by Allah to Babylon where they taught people magic — specifically, magic that destroys marriages. They warn students first that this is a test, but still teach them.

Why this is a problem

Angels in Islam are defined as beings who never disobey Allah (Q 66:6, 16:50). Yet here Allah sends two angels specifically to transmit magical knowledge whose primary use is destroying human marriages. Either Allah commanded them to teach marriage-destroying magic — making Allah the ultimate cause — or the angels disobeyed, contradicting the Quranic definition of angelic nature. Classical commentators invented backstories (Harut and Marut fell from heaven after temptation) that directly contradict Q 66:6.

Why it fails

Either Allah commanded them to teach sorcery (placing divine agency behind something the Quran condemns), or they disobeyed (contradicting angelic nature), or they were not angels (contradicting the passage). Classical commentators recognised the problem and produced competing interpretations — none of which fully resolve the tension the text creates.

Q 2:106 — the abrogation verse creates cascading problems for a perfect book Abrogation Logical Inconsistency Strong Quran 2:106
"We do not abrogate a verse or cause it to be forgotten except that We bring forth [one] better than it or similar to it. Do you not know that Allah is over all things competent?"

What the verse says

Allah can cancel earlier verses and replace them with better ones. This is the foundational verse for the Islamic doctrine of naskh — later verses can override earlier verses.

Why this is a problem

  1. "Better than" implies improvement — an omniscient God's first revelation should already be optimal. Human legislators refine laws over time because they learn; Allah is supposed to be outside time and know all outcomes.
  2. "Cause it to be forgotten" — if the Quran is perfectly preserved, how can Allah have caused verses to be forgotten? Either the Quran is preserved or it is not.

Why it fails

A book claimed to be the eternal unchanging word of an omniscient God cannot honestly be both perfectly preserved and contain verses Allah caused to be forgotten. Classical scholars produced abrogation lists running into the hundreds — and the lists disagree with each other. "Progressive revelation" is exactly what one expects from a human author whose community's needs evolved, not from an eternal being whose wisdom does not change.

"No compulsion in religion" vs "fight until religion is for Allah" — peaceful verses abrogated by their own tradition Contradiction Treatment of Disbelievers Abrogation Strong Quran 2:256 vs 9:5
"There shall be no compulsion in [acceptance of] the religion." (2:256)

"And when the sacred months have passed, then kill the polytheists wherever you find them and capture them and besiege them and sit in wait for them at every place of ambush." (9:5)

What the verses say

The Quran says there is no compulsion in religion; it also commands fighting until all religion is for Allah (Q 2:193) and killing polytheists wherever found (Q 9:5). Classical scholars including al-Suyuti explicitly said Q 9:5 abrogates more than 100 peaceful verses, including Q 2:256.

Why this is a problem

If the classical abrogation doctrine is correct, the peaceful verses modern Muslim apologists quote are, by the tradition's own logic, no longer binding. A divine being who first says "no compulsion" and then commands "kill the polytheists" has either changed His mind, was lying in the first statement, or was lying in the second. None of these preserves an all-knowing God's authorship.

Why it fails

The contextual reading was not the classical reading — al-Suyuti, al-Baghawi, Ibn Kathir, and the Hanafi and Shafi'i schools classified Q 9:5 as the abrogator of tolerance verses. The Q 9:6 escape clause provides a narrow exception; it does not cancel the primary command. Modern jihadist organisations apply the dominant classical hermeneutic — the apologetic rescue requires a modern framework the tradition did not itself deliver.

"Your wives are a place of cultivation" — come to them however you wish Women Strong Quran 2:223
"Your wives are a place of cultivation [i.e., sowing of seed] for you, so come to your place of cultivation however you wish..."

What the verse says

Wives are described as tilled fields (harth) for husbands to sow seed in, approached in any manner the husband wishes. The verse is addressed entirely to the husband; the wife has no grammatical role except as the object of his use.

Why this is a problem

The metaphor reduces women to agricultural property. A field does not consent. Classical legal discussion used "however you wish" to mean any sexual position, with the husband's consent as the only consent that matters — since he is the farmer and she is the land. First-century Paul (1 Cor 7:4) framed marital obligation reciprocally; the Quran, 600 years later, framed it unilaterally.

Why it fails

The verse's grammar places the wife as the object and the husband as the sole grammatical agent. "However you wish" has no textual qualification. Classical jurists read it as permitting any sexual approach, with the wife's preferences absent from the analysis. The "Islamic ethical framework" qualifier is external to the verse — the verse itself encodes the wife as farmland and the husband as farmer, which is precisely the hierarchy the apologetic must apologise for.
